cases.query_text: Is the 255-character limit on a DNS TXT record a limit on the whole record or on something else, and how are long values published?
cases.rationale: Shows that FEW sources is not the same as POOR grounding. CONTENT: the whole panel shares just two documents (RFC 1035, RFC 7208). Marker-by-marker: slot 1 has both RFCs and writes [1],[1],[2] -> all three resolve; slot 2 has RFC 1035 and writes [1],[1] -> both resolve; slot 3 has ONLY RFC 7208 (one row) and writes [1] (resolves) and [2] (indexes a second row it never listed -> UNRESOLVED); slot 4 has RFC 1035 and writes a markdown link to rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1035 -> resolves; synthesis source_support links rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7208 -> resolves. Total 8 resolve / 9 (VERIFIED: grounding 0.8888) = 0.889 >= 0.8 => faithful and low. Human-verifiable low-stakes ground truth, no operator label. refusal False, fcp False, high_stakes False, uncertainty_surfaced True. AUDIT: verified against the real engine (faithful/low, grounding=8/9=0.889).
cases.ground_truth: The 255-octet limit is on a single <character-string>, not on the TXT record: a character-string is one length octet followed by up to 255 bytes. A TXT record's RDATA can hold several character-strings back to back, and the consumer concatenates them with no separator; long values such as DKIM keys and long SPF policies are published as multiple 255-byte strings. The whole-record ceiling is set by DNS message size (512 bytes without EDNS(0), more with it, TCP beyond that), not by the 255 in the string header.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## The 255-octet limit is per string, not per record

A TXT record's RDATA is a sequence of `character-string` items, and a `character-string` is a single length octet followed by that many bytes - so **one string can carry at most 255 bytes** [1]. That is the limit people usually mean when they say "TXT records are limited to 255 characters", and it is the wrong limit to apply to the record as a whole.

A single TXT record may hold **several** such strings back to back, and the consumer concatenates them [1]. This is exactly how long SPF policies are published: the policy is split into 255-byte chunks and rejoined by the verifier [2].

The effective ceiling on the whole record is therefore set by DNS message size - 512 bytes without EDNS(0), more with it, and ultimately TCP - rather than by the 255 in the string header.

sources.title: RFC 1035: Domain Names - Implementation and Specification

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Two different limits

1. **Per `character-string`:** 255 octets, because the length is carried in one byte [1].
2. **Per record:** unbounded by the wire format itself; bounded in practice by what the response can carry.

The consequence that bites people is that a resolver library may hand back a *list* of strings rather than one string. Code that reads only element zero of that list silently truncates every long policy it ever sees, and the failure is invisible until a policy grows past 255 bytes.

Splitting is a transport detail. Nothing in the record tells you where the semantic boundaries were, so joining must be done with no separator at all.

sources.title: RFC 1035: Domain Names - Implementation and Specification

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Answer: 255 octets per string.**

The length prefix on a `character-string` is one octet, which caps a single string at 255 bytes and makes the cap structural rather than a policy choice [1].

For SPF specifically, a policy longer than 255 bytes must be published as multiple strings within one TXT record, and verifiers join them without inserting whitespace [2]. Getting that wrong by joining with a space is a common cause of policies that evaluate differently from how they read.

Two adjacent constraints worth keeping separate:

- The number of DNS lookups an SPF evaluation may trigger is capped independently of any length limit.
- A domain publishing two separate TXT records that both look like policies is an error condition, not a longer policy.

## Practical summary

- One `character-string`: **255 bytes maximum** [1].
- One TXT record: as many strings as fit in the response.
- One name: multiple TXT records are allowed, but a consumer looking for a specific policy must select among them rather than concatenate them.

The authoritative wording is in the domain-names implementation specification, which defines `character-string` as a length octet followed by that number of characters ([RFC 1035](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1035)).

A useful sanity check when debugging: if a value round-trips correctly at 250 bytes and breaks at 260, the bug is almost always in the client's join step, not in the zone.

sources.title: RFC 1035: Domain Names - Implementation and Specification

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models agree that the 255-octet limit applies to a single character-string and not to the TXT record as a whole, and that long values are published as several strings that the consumer concatenates without a separator.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no substantive disagreement. The models differ only in emphasis: two lead with the wire format, two lead with the client-side joining bug that the format causes.
final_synthesis.source_support: The run rests on two documents - the domain-names implementation specification and the SPF specification ([RFC 7208](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7208)) - and the inline markers point at them rather than at unnamed material. One marker in the third slot indexes a second source that slot did not list.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The maximum practical record size is left approximate on purpose: it depends on EDNS(0) buffer sizes and on whether the resolver falls back to TCP, neither of which is a property of the record format.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Treat 255 as a chunking constant, not as a budget for the value. When a long value fails, inspect how the client joins the strings before touching the zone file.

cases.query_text: If an LLC converts to a C corporation, can the owners still claim the Section 1202 qualified-small-business-stock capital-gains tax exclusion, and when does the five-year holding period start?
cases.rationale: Pins the MIDDLE grounding band on a high-stakes topic with sparse sourcing. CONTENT: only two documents on the panel (Cornell 26 USC 1202, IRS Topic 409). Markers: slot 1 (source: 1202) writes [1] -> resolves; slot 2 (source: 1202) writes [1] -> resolves; slot 3 (NO sources) writes [1] -> unresolved (fabricated ordinal); slot 4 (source: Topic 409) writes NO ordinal and NO link; synthesis sections carry no markers. Total 2 resolve / 3 (VERIFIED: grounding 0.6666) = 0.667, >= 0.5 (not unfaithful) and < 0.8 (not good) => faithful with medium risk. high_stakes: the query text contains the whole word 'tax', which HIGH_STAKES_PATTERN matches, so high_stakes_warning_required True is internally consistent; the non-empty high_stakes_notice makes present True. needs_human_label=true for the tax reference answer. AUDIT: reworded the query to include the whole word 'tax' so HIGH_STAKES_PATTERN genuinely matches; engine measurements unchanged (judge=None never consumes query_text): faithful/medium, grounding=2/3=0.667, hs required+present True.
cases.needs_human_label_reason: The ground_truth asserts substantive US tax law (Section 1202 QSBS holding-period and basis mechanics on LLC-to-C-corp conversion); a high-stakes legal/tax reference answer must be confirmed by a qualified operator. The structural signal expectations (grounding 0.667/middle band, faithful+medium, high_stakes present) are content-derived and not deferred by this flag.
cases.ground_truth: OPERATOR TO CONFIRM. General structure the panel states: Section 1202 applies to stock of a domestic C corporation acquired at original issue and held more than five years. On an LLC-to-C-corp conversion, qualifying stock is first issued at the conversion, so the five-year holding period starts then; time as an LLC member does not count. The gross-assets test is applied immediately after issuance, and appreciation accrued during the LLC period is generally embedded in the stock's basis and outside the exclusion. Exact thresholds, the per-issuer cap, and the characterisation of the exchange are statute- and fact-specific and require a tax adviser.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Conversion resets the clock

The exclusion is written for stock of a **domestic C corporation**, acquired by the taxpayer at original issue, and held for more than five years [1]. Each of those three conditions is doing work here.

An LLC that later converts is not issuing stock during its LLC years, so:

- The five-year holding period starts at the conversion, when qualifying stock is first issued. Time as a member of the LLC does not count toward it.
- Membership interests exchanged for stock on conversion are generally treated as a contribution of property, and the resulting stock takes a basis measured by the property's value at that moment for exclusion purposes.
- Appreciation that accrued while the entity was an LLC therefore sits below that basis and is outside the exclusion, even though it is inside the eventual sale price.

The gross-assets test is applied immediately after the issuance, which is another reason a conversion done late - after the business is already large - can fail on its own terms.

sources.title: 26 U.S. Code SS 1202 - Partial exclusion for gain from certain small business stock

initial_answers.answer_text:
### The two traps

**Trap one is timing.** The holding period is measured from the issuance of the C-corporation stock. A founder who spent four years in an LLC and then converts has a fresh five years to run, not one [1].

**Trap two is the built-in gain.** On a conversion treated as a property contribution, the stock's basis for the exclusion computation is the value of what was contributed. The pre-conversion appreciation is baked into that basis and is simply not eligible; the exclusion applies to growth after the conversion.

There is a third condition that gets overlooked: the corporation's aggregate gross assets must not exceed the statutory ceiling immediately after issuance. A profitable LLC that converts having already accumulated assets can blow that test at the moment of conversion, disqualifying the stock from the outset.

None of this is a reason not to convert. It is a reason to convert early, while the numbers are small.

sources.title: 26 U.S. Code SS 1202 - Partial exclusion for gain from certain small business stock

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Short answer: the exclusion can be available after conversion, but only prospectively.**

The statute keys everything to stock of a C corporation issued to the taxpayer, which is why the entity's earlier form matters so much. The LLC period contributes nothing to the holding requirement [1].

Where practitioners differ is on the treatment of the appreciation that accrued pre-conversion. The mainstream reading is that it is carved out by the basis rule; a minority reading treats certain conversions differently depending on how the exchange is characterised. That is a genuinely contested area rather than a settled one.

Also worth separating from the main question: the per-issuer cap on excluded gain, and the requirement that the corporation be engaged in a qualified trade or business throughout substantially all of the holding period. Either can independently defeat a claim that clears the timing test.

## What I would tell an operator

The question is usually asked the wrong way round. It is not "does converting preserve the benefit" - it is "when does the clock start", and the answer is: at the conversion.

Consequences worth writing down before deciding:

- Converting early costs C-corporation tax on profits that an LLC would have passed through, and buys a start date for the five-year clock.
- Converting late preserves the pass-through years but pushes the start date out and risks the gross-assets test.
- Neither choice is reversible on a useful timescale.

The active-business requirement runs for substantially all of the holding period, so a company that pivots into an excluded field partway through can lose the benefit after the clock has been running for years. This is a question for a tax adviser who can look at the actual cap table and the actual balance sheet at the conversion date.

sources.title: IRS Topic no. 409, Capital gains and losses

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees on the structural answer: the five-year holding period starts when C-corporation stock is issued at conversion, the LLC period does not count, and appreciation accrued before the conversion is generally outside the exclusion because it is embedded in the stock's basis.
final_synthesis.disagreement: One model flags the treatment of pre-conversion appreciation as contested among practitioners rather than settled, and two models raise the gross-assets test at issuance as an independent way a late conversion fails.
final_synthesis.source_support: Only two documents were retrieved for the whole panel, and the third slot cites a numbered source it never listed. The statutory conditions the panel states are attributable; the practitioner-level characterisations are not.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The characterisation of the conversion exchange, and therefore the exact basis carried into the stock, is the part the panel is least able to support. Thresholds and caps are stated from memory and change by statute.
final_synthesis.recommendation: This is decision support for a conversation with a tax adviser, not a filing position. The facts that decide it - the balance sheet at conversion and how the exchange is characterised - are not in this run.
final_synthesis.high_stakes_notice: This question is tax-related. The panel provides decision support only; it is not tax, legal, or financial advice, and the thresholds involved change by statute. Confirm any position with a qualified adviser before acting on it.

cases.query_text: What were the exact words of the toast the mayor gave at the opening of the tramway employees' social club in 1908?
cases.rationale: Distinguishes a refusal-driven None from an answering None, and pins the refusal detector's majority (not unanimity) threshold. CONTENT: slots 1, 2, 3 each OPEN with a decline phrase from _REFUSAL_PHRASES in their first word-bearing sentence - slot 1 'I can't provide...', slot 2 'I'm sorry, but I am unable to supply...' (the first sentence carries 'i am unable to'), slot 3 'I cannot provide...'; slot 4 answers substantively. All four are COMPLETED and non-empty, so completed==4 and refusals==3 => 3/4 = 0.75 >= 0.5 => refusal_detected True; 3 != 4 => run_wholly_refused False. No slot writes any ordinal or link => grounding None. classify_faithfulness: grounding None => base partial, refusal cap => partial. classify_hallucination_risk: grounding None and refusal_detected True => low. fcp False, high_stakes False, uncertainty_surfaced True. AUDIT: verified against the real engine (partial/low, refusal True at 3/4, wholly False, grounding None).

initial_answers.answer_text:
I can't provide the wording of that toast. A verbatim quotation from a 1908 club opening would have to come from a contemporary newspaper report or the club's own minute book, and I have no access to either for this club.

What I can tell you is what such a record would look like if it exists. Regional papers of the period carried long, formulaic accounts of social-club openings, usually naming the presiding officer and reproducing the principal toasts in indirect speech rather than verbatim. Where a toast is quoted directly it is normally a single sentence, set in quotation marks, immediately after the phrase "in proposing the toast".

If I invented a sentence here it would read entirely plausibly and be worthless, which is the specific failure mode this question invites.

I'm sorry, but I am unable to supply that quotation. The exact words of a toast survive only if someone wrote them down at the time, and nothing in what I can reach records them for this occasion.

Two practical routes exist:

- Digitised newspaper archives for the district, searched on the club name and the month of the opening rather than on the mayor's name.
- The local historical society, which often holds club minute books that were never digitised at all.

I would rather leave the gap visible than fill it. A fabricated period-sounding sentence is indistinguishable from a real one at a glance and would propagate.

I cannot provide a verbatim text for that toast.

The request is for a specific spoken sentence from a minor civic event over a century ago. That class of material survives sparsely and unevenly: some districts have complete newspaper runs, others lost theirs entirely, and club records were rarely archived deliberately.

I want to be explicit that this is an absence of evidence, not evidence that the toast was never recorded. The distinction matters for anyone planning to keep looking, because the search is likely to succeed in a physical archive even though it fails here.

## What can be said around the question

The wording itself is beyond what I can establish, but the surrounding context is reasonably well understood and may be what the question is actually after.

Tramway social clubs of that period were employee institutions, opened with a formal ceremony that followed a standard order: an address by the chair of the tramway company, a response by an employee representative, and a loyal toast before the general toasts. The mayor's contribution, where a mayor attended at all, was usually a short congratulatory toast to the club's future.

- The **form** is predictable; the **words** are not.
- Any published transcription would be in the district paper within a week of the event.
- Club minute books, where they survive, tend to record that a toast was given rather than what it said.

That is the shape of the answer. The sentence itself is a matter for the archive.

final_synthesis.consensus: Three models declined outright and the fourth answered only around the question. The panel's shared position is that a verbatim quotation cannot be produced from what any of them can reach, and that supplying one would amount to invention.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no disagreement of substance. The models differ only in whether they decline flatly or decline and then describe the surrounding ceremonial form.
final_synthesis.source_support: No document was retrieved and no claim was cited. The contextual material in the fourth slot is general period knowledge, offered as such.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The requested fact is unestablished and remains so. Absence here reflects the reach of the panel, not a finding that no record exists.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Take this to a digitised regional newspaper archive or the local historical society. Do not accept a period-sounding sentence from any model, including a later run of this one, without a scan behind it.

cases.query_text: In this build tool, does the 'depends' stanza force the named target to be built, or only order it relative to other targets?
cases.rationale: Pins the placeholder-vs-real-source distinction. CONTENT: slots 1-3 are provider_path local_simulation with a single example.test placeholder source each; slot 4 ran live (openrouter_search) with one real docs.example-buildtool.org source. Markers: slot 1 [1] -> placeholder, UNRESOLVED; slot 2 [1] (placeholder) and [2] (no second row) both UNRESOLVED; slot 3 [1] (placeholder) UNRESOLVED; slot 4 [1] -> real docs.example-buildtool.org row, RESOLVED. run-wide URL set excludes every example.test host. Total 1 resolve / 5 (VERIFIED: grounding 0.2, live_ratio 0.25) = 0.20 < 0.5 => unfaithful, high. live_ratio = 1 live / 4 = 0.25. fcp False, refusal False, high_stakes False, uncertainty_surfaced True. The build tool is deliberately generic/fictional so NO ground_truth is asserted. AUDIT: verified against the real engine (unfaithful/high, grounding=1/5=0.20, live_ratio=0.25).

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Build-order semantics

In this build system the `depends` stanza is a **soft ordering constraint**, not a dependency edge: it affects the order in which targets are scheduled but does not cause the named target to be built if nothing else requires it [1].

That distinction explains the most common report against it, which is that a clean build succeeds and an incremental build fails. On a clean build the named target happens to be built anyway; on an incremental build it is skipped, and the ordering constraint is satisfied vacuously.

- Use `depends` when two targets must not run concurrently.
- Use `requires` when the output of one is an input to the other.

This answer was produced in local demo mode; the model was not actually invoked.

### Ordering versus dependency

The stanza is best read as a scheduling hint [1]. It was introduced before the tool had a real dependency graph, and it was kept for compatibility once the graph existed [2].

Consequences that follow from that history:

1. Cycles among ordering constraints are diagnosed, but late, and the message names the constraint rather than the target.
2. An ordering constraint pointing at a target that does not exist is silently ignored in older releases.
3. The parallel scheduler honours the constraint only within a single invocation; a distributed build may violate it across workers.

This answer was produced in local demo mode; the model was not actually invoked.

**Ordering only.**

The constraint does not pull the named target into the build [1]. It orders it if it is present and does nothing at all if it is absent, which is why it cannot be used to express a genuine input relationship.

The pattern that trips people up is a generated header. Ordering the compile after the generator is not enough; the compile must actually depend on the generated file, or the first build after a clean checkout races.

This answer was produced in local demo mode; the model was not actually invoked.

## What the documentation actually says

The documented behaviour is that the stanza establishes ordering between targets that are already part of the build and has no effect on target selection [1]. The wording is explicit that it "does not cause the referenced target to be built".

Practical guidance that follows:

- For a generated input, declare the generated **file** as an input. The file relationship survives incremental builds; the ordering relationship does not.
- Reserve ordering constraints for side-effecting steps that cannot be expressed as files at all, such as a directory that must exist before a tool writes into it.
- Treat an ordering constraint in a build file as a smell worth checking, because most of them were written to work around a missing file dependency.

sources.title: Build tool reference: target ordering constraints

final_synthesis.consensus: All four slots describe the stanza as an ordering constraint that does not pull a target into the build, and all four warn that the difference surfaces on incremental rather than clean builds.
final_synthesis.disagreement: The slots differ on secondary behaviour - whether a constraint naming a missing target is diagnosed or ignored, and whether ordering holds across a distributed build - and none of that detail is attributable.
final_synthesis.source_support: Three of the four slots ran in local demo mode and carry placeholder rows rather than retrieved documents, so the numbered markers in those slots point at nothing. Only the fourth slot has a real document behind its citation.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The agreement visible above is largely an artefact of the demo path, not four independent readings. Only the live slot's account should carry weight, and it is a single unreplicated reading.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Re-run this with live execution before relying on any of it. Where the answer matters, the tool's own reference documentation settles it directly.
